subject: science
description: Ask questions to classify objects according to those seen in the day sky, the night sky, and both.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate observations about time patterns (day to night and night to day) and objects (sun, moon, stars) in the day and night sky.

subject: science
description: Develop a model to communicate the changes that occur in the sky during the day, as day turns into night, during the night, and as night turns into day using pictures and words. (Clarification statement: Students are not expected to understand tilt of the Earth, rotation, or revolution.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate observations about time patterns (day to night and night to day) and objects (sun, moon, stars) in the day and night sky.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to identify and describe earth materials—soil, rocks, water, and air.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to describe the physical attributes of earth materials (soil, rocks, water, and air).

subject: science
description: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how rocks can be grouped by physical attributes (size, weight, texture, color).
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to describe the physical attributes of earth materials (soil, rocks, water, and air).

subject: science
description: Use tools to observe and record physical attributes of soil such as texture and color.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to describe the physical attributes of earth materials (soil, rocks, water, and air).

subject: science
description: Ask questions to compare and sort objects made of different materials. (Common materials include clay, cloth, plastic, wood, paper, and metal.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to describe objects in terms of the materials they are made of and their physical attributes.

subject: science
description: Use senses and science tools to classify common objects, such as buttons or swatches of cloth, according to their physical attributes (color, size, shape, weight, and texture).
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to describe objects in terms of the materials they are made of and their physical attributes.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out an investigation to predict and observe whether objects, based on their physical attributes, will sink or float.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to describe objects in terms of the materials they are made of and their physical attributes.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out an investigation to determine the relationship between an object’s physical attributes and its resulting motion (straight, circular, back and forth, fast and slow, and motionless) when a force is applied. (Examples could include toss, drop, push, and pull.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to compare and describe different types of motion.

subject: science
description: Construct an argument as to the best way to move an object based on its physical attributes.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to compare and describe different types of motion.

subject: science
description: Construct an explanation based on observations to recognize the differences between organisms and nonliving objects.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about how organisms (alive and not alive) and non-living objects are grouped.

subject: science
description: Develop a model to represent how a set of organisms and nonliving objects are sorted into groups based on their attributes.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about how organisms (alive and not alive) and non-living objects are grouped.

subject: science
description: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how animals can be grouped according to their features.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to compare the similarities and differences in groups of organisms.

subject: science
description: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how plants can be grouped according to their features.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to compare the similarities and differences in groups of organisms.

subject: science
description: Ask questions and make observations to identify the similarities and differences of offspring to their parents and to other members of the same species.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to compare the similarities and differences in groups of organisms.

subject: science
description: Represent data in tables and/or graphs to identify and describe different types of weather and the characteristics of each type.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate weather data to identify weather patterns.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to identify forms of precipitation such as rain, snow, sleet, and hailstones as either solid (ice) or liquid (water).
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate weather data to identify weather patterns.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out investigations on current weather conditions by observing, measuring with simple weather instruments (thermometer, wind vane, rain gauge), and recording weather data (temperature, precipitation, sky conditions, and weather events) in a periodic journal, on a calendar, and graphically.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate weather data to identify weather patterns.

subject: science
description: Analyze data to identify seasonal patterns of change. (Clarification statement: Examples could include temperature, rainfall/snowfall, and changes to the environment.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate weather data to identify weather patterns.

subject: science
description: Use observations to construct an explanation of how light is required to make objects visible.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to investigate light and sound.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to identify and compare sources of light.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to investigate light and sound.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out an investigation of shadows by placing objects at various points from a source of light.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to investigate light and sound.

subject: science
description: Construct an explanation supported by evidence that vibrating materials can make sound and that sound can make materials vibrate.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to investigate light and sound.

subject: science
description: Design a signal that can serve as an emergency alert using light and/or sound to communicate over a distance.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to investigate light and sound.

subject: science
description: Construct an explanation of how magnets are used in everyday life. (Clarification statement: Everyday life uses could include refrigerator magnets, toys, magnetic latches, and name tags.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to demonstrate the effects of magnets on other magnets and other objects.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out an investigation to demonstrate how magnets attract and repel each other and the effect of magnets on common objects.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to demonstrate the effects of magnets on other magnets and other objects.

subject: science
description: Develop models to identify the parts of a plant—root, stem, leaf, and flower.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the basic needs of plants and animals.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to compare and contrast the basic needs of plants (air, water, light, and nutrients) and animals (air, water, food, and shelter).
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the basic needs of plants and animals.

subject: science
description: Design a solution to ensure that a plant or animal has all of its needs met.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the basic needs of plants and animals.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to describe the physical attributes (size and brightness) of stars.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about stars having different sizes and brightness.

subject: science
description: Construct an argument to support the claim that although the sun appears to be the brightest and largest star, it is actually medium in size and brightness.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about stars having different sizes and brightness.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out an investigation to determine the effect of the position of the sun in relation to a fixed object on Earth at various times of the day.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to develop an understanding of the patterns of the sun and the moon and the sun’s effect on Earth.

subject: science
description: Design and build a structure that demonstrates how shadows change throughout the day.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to develop an understanding of the patterns of the sun and the moon and the sun’s effect on Earth.

subject: science
description: Represent data in tables and/or graphs of the length of the day and night to recognize the change in seasons.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to develop an understanding of the patterns of the sun and the moon and the sun’s effect on Earth.

subject: science
description: Use data from personal observations to describe, illustrate, and predict how the appearance of the moon changes over time in a pattern. (Clarification statement: Students are not required to know the names of the phases of the moon or understand the tilt of the Earth.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to develop an understanding of the patterns of the sun and the moon and the sun’s effect on Earth.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to obtain information about major changes to the environment in your community.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about how weather, plants, animals, and humans cause changes to the environment. (Clarification statement: Changes should be easily observable and could be seen on school grounds or at home.)

subject: science
description: Construct an explanation of the causes and effects of a change to the environment in your community.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about how weather, plants, animals, and humans cause changes to the environment. (Clarification statement: Changes should be easily observable and could be seen on school grounds or at home.)

subject: science
description: Ask questions to describe and classify different objects according to their physical properties. (Clarification statement: Examples of physical properties could include color, mass, length, texture, hardness, strength, absorbency, and flexibility.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the properties of matter and changes that occur in objects.

subject: science
description: Construct an explanation for how structures made from small pieces (linking cubes, building blocks) can be disassembled and then rearranged to make new and different structures.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the properties of matter and changes that occur in objects.

subject: science
description: Provide evidence from observations to construct an explanation that some changes in matter caused by heating or cooling can be reversed and some changes are irreversible. (Clarification statement: Changes in matter could include heating or freezing of water, baking a cake, boiling an egg.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the properties of matter and changes that occur in objects.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out an investigation to demonstrate how pushing and pulling on an object affects the motion of the object.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to explain the effect of a force (a push or a pull) in the movement of an object (changes in speed and direction).

subject: science
description: Design a device to change the speed or direction of an object.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to explain the effect of a force (a push or a pull) in the movement of an object (changes in speed and direction).

subject: science
description: Record and analyze data to decide if a design solution works as intended to change the speed or direction of an object with a force (a push or a pull).
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to explain the effect of a force (a push or a pull) in the movement of an object (changes in speed and direction).

subject: science
description: Ask questions to determine the sequence of the life cycle of common animals in your area: a mammal such as a cat, dog or classroom pet, a bird such as a chicken, an amphibian such as a frog, and an insect such as a butterfly.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the life cycles of different living organisms.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out an investigation of the life cycle of a plant by growing a plant from a seed and by recording changes over a period of time.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the life cycles of different living organisms.

subject: science
description: Construct an explanation of an animal’s role in dispersing seeds or in the pollination of plants.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the life cycles of different living organisms.

subject: science
description: Develop models to illustrate the unique and diverse life cycles of organisms other than humans.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the life cycles of different living organisms.

subject: science
description: Ask questions and analyze data to classify rocks by their physical attributes (color, texture, luster, and hardness) using simple tests. (Clarification statement: Mohs scale should be studied at this level. Cleavage, streak and the classification of rocks as sedimentary, igneous, and metamorphic are studied in sixth grade.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the physical attributes of rocks and soils.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out investigations to describe properties (color, texture, capacity to retain water, and ability to support growth of plants) of soils and soil types (sand, clay, loam).
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the physical attributes of rocks and soils.

subject: science
description: Make observations of the local environment to construct an explanation of how water and/or wind have made changes to soil and/or rocks over time. (Clarification statement: Examples could include ripples in dirt on a playground and a hole formed under gutters.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the physical attributes of rocks and soils.

subject: science
description: Construct an argument from observations of fossils (authentic or reproductions) to communicate how they serve as evidence of past organisms and the environments in which they lived.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information on how fossils provide evidence of past organisms.

subject: science
description: Develop a model to describe the sequence and conditions required for an organism to become fossilized. (Clarification statement: Types of fossils (cast, mold, trace, and true) are not addressed in this standard.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information on how fossils provide evidence of past organisms.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to identify sources of heat energy. (Clarification statement: Examples could include sunlight, friction, and burning.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the ways heat energy is transferred and measured.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out an investigation to gather data using thermometers to produce tables and charts that illustrate the effect of sunlight on various objects. (Clarification statement: The use of both Fahrenheit and Celsius temperature scales is expected.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the ways heat energy is transferred and measured.

subject: science
description: Use tools and every day materials to design and construct a device/structure that will increase/decrease the warming effects of sunlight on various materials. (Clarification statement: Conduction, convection, and radiation are taught in upper grades.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the ways heat energy is transferred and measured.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to differentiate between plants, animals, and habitats found within Georgia’s geographic regions.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the similarities and differences between plants, animals, and habitats found within geographic regions (Blue Ridge Mountains, Piedmont, Coastal Plains, Valley and Ridge, and Appalachian Plateau) of Georgia.

subject: science
description: Construct an explanation of how external features and adaptations (camouflage, hibernation, migration, mimicry) of animals allow them to survive in their habitat.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the similarities and differences between plants, animals, and habitats found within geographic regions (Blue Ridge Mountains, Piedmont, Coastal Plains, Valley and Ridge, and Appalachian Plateau) of Georgia.

subject: science
description: Use evidence to construct an explanation of why some organisms can thrive in one habitat and not in another.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the similarities and differences between plants, animals, and habitats found within geographic regions (Blue Ridge Mountains, Piedmont, Coastal Plains, Valley and Ridge, and Appalachian Plateau) of Georgia.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to collect information and create records of sources and effects of pollution on the plants and animals.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the effects of pollution (air, land, and water) and humans on the environment.

subject: science
description: Explore, research, and communicate solutions, such as conservation of resources and recycling of materials, to protect plants and animals.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the effects of pollution (air, land, and water) and humans on the environment.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to compare and contrast technological advances that have changed the amount and type of information on distant objects in the sky.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to compare and contrast the physical attributes of stars and planets.

subject: science
description: Construct an argument on why some stars (including the Earth’s sun) appear to be larger or brighter than others. (Clarification statement: Differences are limited to distance and size, not age or stage of evolution.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to compare and contrast the physical attributes of stars and planets.

subject: science
description: Construct an explanation of the differences between stars and planets.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to compare and contrast the physical attributes of stars and planets.

subject: science
description: Evaluate strengths and limitations of models of our solar system in describing relative size, order, appearance and composition of planets and the sun. (Clarification statement: Composition of planets is limited to rocky vs. gaseous.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to compare and contrast the physical attributes of stars and planets.

subject: science
description: Develop a model to support an explanation of why the length of day and night change throughout the year.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to model the effects of the position and motion of the Earth and the moon in relation to the sun as observed from the Earth.

subject: science
description: Develop a model based on observations to describe the repeating pattern of the phases of the moon (new, crescent, quarter, gibbous, and full).
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to model the effects of the position and motion of the Earth and the moon in relation to the sun as observed from the Earth.

subject: science
description: Construct an explanation of how the Earth’s orbit, with its consistent tilt, affects seasonal changes.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to model the effects of the position and motion of the Earth and the moon in relation to the sun as observed from the Earth.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out investigations to observe the flow of energy in water as it changes states from solid (ice) to liquid (water) to gas (water vapor) and changes from gas to liquid to solid.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to demonstrate the water cycle.

subject: science
description: Develop models to illustrate multiple pathways water may take during the water cycle (evaporation, condensation, and precipitation). (Clarification statement: Students should understand that the water cycle does not follow a single pathway.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to demonstrate the water cycle.

subject: science
description: Construct an explanation of how weather instruments (thermometer, rain gauge, barometer, wind vane, and anemometer) are used in gathering weather data and making forecasts.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to predict weather events and infer weather patterns using weather charts/maps and collected weather data.

subject: science
description: Interpret data from weather maps, including fronts (warm, cold, and stationary), temperature, pressure, and precipitation to make an informed prediction about tomorrow’s weather.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to predict weather events and infer weather patterns using weather charts/maps and collected weather data.

subject: science
description: Ask questions and use observations of cloud types (cirrus, stratus, and cumulus) and data of weather conditions to predict weather events.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to predict weather events and infer weather patterns using weather charts/maps and collected weather data.

subject: science
description: Construct an explanation based on research to communicate the difference between weather and climate.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to predict weather events and infer weather patterns using weather charts/maps and collected weather data.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out investigations to observe and record how light interacts with various materials to classify them as opaque, transparent, or translucent.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the nature of light and how light interacts with objects.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out investigations to describe the path light travels from a light source to a mirror and how it is reflected by the mirror using different angles.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the nature of light and how light interacts with objects.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out an investigation utilizing everyday materials to explore examples of when light is refracted. (Clarification statement: Everyday materials could include prisms, eyeglasses, and a glass of water.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the nature of light and how light interacts with objects.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out an investigation utilizing everyday objects to produce sound and predict the effects of changing the strength or speed of vibrations.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about how sound is produced and changed and how sound and/or light can be used to communicate.

subject: science
description: Design and construct a device to communicate across a distance using light and/or sound.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about how sound is produced and changed and how sound and/or light can be used to communicate.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out an investigation on the effects of balanced and unbalanced forces on an object and communicate the results.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the relationship between balanced and unbalanced forces.

subject: science
description: Construct an argument to support the claim that gravitational force affects the motion of an object.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the relationship between balanced and unbalanced forces.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to identify and explain the uses of simple machines (lever, pulley, wedge, inclined plane, wheel and axle, and screw) and how forces are changed when simple machines are used to complete tasks. (Clarification statement: The use of mathematical formulas is not expected.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the relationship between balanced and unbalanced forces.

subject: science
description: Develop a model to describe the roles of producers, consumers, and decomposers in a community. (Clarification statement: Students are not expected to identify the different types of consumers – herbivores, carnivores, omnivores and scavengers.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the roles of organisms and the flow of energy within an ecosystem.

subject: science
description: Develop simple models to illustrate the flow of energy through a food web/food chain beginning with sunlight and including producers, consumers, and decomposers.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the roles of organisms and the flow of energy within an ecosystem.

subject: science
description: Design a scenario to demonstrate the effect of a change on an ecosystem. (Clarification statement: Include living and non-living factors in the scenario.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the roles of organisms and the flow of energy within an ecosystem.

subject: science
description: Use printed and digital data to develop a model illustrating and describing changes to the flow of energy in an ecosystem when plants or animals become scarce, extinct or over-abundant.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the roles of organisms and the flow of energy within an ecosystem.

subject: science
description: Construct an argument supported by scientific evidence to identify surface features (examples could include deltas, sand dunes, mountains, volcanoes) as being caused by constructive and/or destructive processes (examples could include deposition, weathering, erosion, and impact of organisms).
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to identify surface features on the Earth caused by constructive and/or destructive processes.

subject: science
description: Develop simple interactive models to collect data that illustrate how changes in surface features are/were caused by constructive and/or destructive processes.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to identify surface features on the Earth caused by constructive and/or destructive processes.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to obtain information on how technology is used to limit and/or predict the impact of constructive and destructive processes. (Clarification statement: Examples could include seismological studies, flood forecasting (GIS maps), engineering/construction methods and materials, and infrared/satellite imagery.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to identify surface features on the Earth caused by constructive and/or destructive processes.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out investigations of physical changes by manipulating, separating and mixing dry and liquid materials.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to explain the differences between a physical change and a chemical change.

subject: science
description: Construct an argument based on observations to support a claim that the physical changes in the state of water are due to temperature changes, which cause small particles that cannot be seen to move differently.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to explain the differences between a physical change and a chemical change.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out an investigation to determine if a chemical change occurred based on observable evidence (color, gas, temperature change, odor, new substance produced).
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to explain the differences between a physical change and a chemical change.

subject: science
description: Obtain and combine information from multiple sources to explain the difference between naturally occurring electricity (static) and human-harnessed electricity.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to investigate electricity.

subject: science
description: Design a complete, simple electric circuit, and explain all necessary components.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to investigate electricity.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out investigations on common materials to determine if they are insulators or conductors of electricity.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to investigate electricity.

subject: science
description: Construct an argument based on experimental evidence to communicate the differences in function and purpose of an electromagnet and a magnet. (Clarification statement: Function is limited to understanding temporary and permanent magnetism.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about magnetism and its relationship to electricity.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out an investigation to observe the interaction between a magnetic field and a magnetic object. (Clarification statement: The interaction should include placing materials of various types (wood, paper, glass, metal, and rocks) and thickness between the magnet and the magnetic object.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about magnetism and its relationship to electricity.

subject: science
description: Develop a model that illustrates how animals are sorted into groups (vertebrate and invertebrate) and how vertebrates are sorted into groups (fish, amphibian, reptile, bird, and mammal) using data from multiple sources.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to group organisms using scientific classification procedures.

subject: science
description: Develop a model that illustrates how plants are sorted into groups (seed producers, non-seed producers) using data from multiple sources.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to group organisms using scientific classification procedures.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to compare and contrast instincts and learned behaviors.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information showing that some characteristics of organisms are inherited and other characteristics are acquired.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to compare and contrast inherited and acquired physical traits. (Clarification statement: Punnett squares and genetics are taught in future grades.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information showing that some characteristics of organisms are inherited and other characteristics are acquired.

subject: science
description: Gather evidence by utilizing technology tools to support a claim that plants and animals are comprised of cells too small to be seen without magnification.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to compare and contrast the parts of plant and animal cells.

subject: science
description: Develop a model to identify and label parts of a plant cell (membrane, wall, cytoplasm, nucleus, chloroplasts) and of an animal cell (membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus).
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to compare and contrast the parts of plant and animal cells.

subject: science
description: Construct an explanation that differentiates between the structure of plant and animal cells.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to compare and contrast the parts of plant and animal cells.

subject: science
description: Construct an argument using scientific evidence to support a claim that some microorganisms are beneficial.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about how microorganisms benefit or harm larger organisms. (Clarification statement: Possible microorganisms could include Tardigrades, Lactobacillus, Probiotics, Rotifers, Salmonella, Clostridium botulinum (Botox), E-coli, Algae, etc. Students are not expected to know these specific microorganisms. The list is provided to give teachers examples.)

subject: science
description: Construct an argument using scientific evidence to support a claim that some microorganisms are harmful.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about how microorganisms benefit or harm larger organisms. (Clarification statement: Possible microorganisms could include Tardigrades, Lactobacillus, Probiotics, Rotifers, Salmonella, Clostridium botulinum (Botox), E-coli, Algae, etc. Students are not expected to know these specific microorganisms. The list is provided to give teachers examples.)

subject: science
description: Ask questions to determine changes in models of Earth’s position in the solar system, and origins of the universe as evidence that scientific theories change with the addition of new information. (Clarification statement: Students should consider Earth’s position in geocentric and heliocentric models and the Big Bang as it describes the formation of the universe.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about current scientific views of the universe and how those views evolved.

subject: science
description: Develop a model to represent the position of the solar system in the Milky Way galaxy and in the known universe.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about current scientific views of the universe and how those views evolved.

subject: science
description: Analyze and interpret data to compare and contrast the planets in our solar system in terms of:  size relative to Earth,  surface and atmospheric features,  relative distance from the sun, and  ability to support life.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about current scientific views of the universe and how those views evolved.

subject: science
description: Develop and use a model to explain the interaction of gravity and inertia that governs the motion of objects in the solar system.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about current scientific views of the universe and how those views evolved.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to compare and contrast the characteristics, composition, and location of comets, asteroids, and meteoroids.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about current scientific views of the universe and how those views evolved.

subject: science
description: Develop and use a model to demonstrate the phases of the moon by showing the relative positions of the sun, Earth, and moon.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the effects of the relative positions of the sun, Earth, and moon.

subject: science
description: Construct an explanation of the cause of solar and lunar eclipses.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the effects of the relative positions of the sun, Earth, and moon.

subject: science
description: Analyze and interpret data to relate the tilt of the Earth to the distribution of sunlight throughout the year and its effect on seasons.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about the effects of the relative positions of the sun, Earth, and moon.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to determine where water is located on Earth’s surface (oceans, rivers, lakes, swamps, groundwater, aquifers, and ice) and communicate the relative proportion of water at each location.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to recognize the significant role of water in Earth processes.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out an investigation to illustrate the role of the sun’s energy in atmospheric conditions that lead to the cycling of water. (Clarification statement: The water cycle should include evaporation, condensation, precipitation, transpiration, infiltration, groundwater, and runoff.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to recognize the significant role of water in Earth processes.

subject: science
description: Ask questions to identify and communicate, using graphs and maps, the composition, location, and subsurface topography of the world’s oceans.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to recognize the significant role of water in Earth processes.

subject: science
description: Analyze and interpret data to create graphic representations of the causes and effects of waves, currents, and tides in Earth’s systems.
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to recognize the significant role of water in Earth processes.

subject: science
description: Analyze and interpret data to compare and contrast the composition of Earth’s atmospheric layers (including the ozone layer) and greenhouse gases. (Clarification statement: Earth’s atmospheric layers include the troposphere, stratosphere, mesosphere, and thermosphere.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about how the sun, land, and water affect climate and weather.

subject: science
description: Plan and carry out an investigation to demonstrate how energy from the sun transfers heat to air, land and water at different rates. (Clarification statement: Heat transfer should include the processes of conduction, convection, and radiation.)
stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information about how the sun, land, and water affect climate and weather.

subject: science

stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to describe how cell structures, cells, tissues, organs, and organ systems interact to maintain the basic needs of organisms.

subject: science

stem: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to explain how organisms reproduce either sexually or asexually and transfer genetic information to determine the traits of their offspring.
